skill.name: commit-prepare
skill.description: Prepare Conventional Commits messages from staged and unstaged git changes, with review steps before staging or committing.
skill.summary: Draft and confirm Conventional Commits messages from local git diffs.
skill.author: ByronWilliamsCPA

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell patterns because the skill documents git commands and commit-message examples. Review found no malicious intent, data exfiltration, prompt injection, or hidden execution path, but the skill does instruct agents to run repository-mutating git commands after user confirmation.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Mutating Git Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to inspect diffs, stage files, and run git commit after user confirmation. This is legitimate for a commit-preparation skill, but it can modify repository state and create commits, so users should review staged files and the final message before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and semantically tied to the skill purpose. Risk is operational rather than malicious because the file also requires user confirmation before committing.

low_findings.title: Documentation Link Flagged as Network Usage
low_findings.description: The hardcoded Claude Code URL appears only in attribution text, not in code that performs a network request. I did not find evidence of data transmission, credential collection, or a remote callback.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both URL locations are inside Markdown attribution examples. There is no fetch, curl, webhook, or other network execution instruction at those lines.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography matches at the reported lines do not reference cryptographic algorithms or hashing APIs. The lines contain the skill description, commit-message body text, and an example sentence about retry backoff.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain Markdown prose. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or crypto API usage in the skill file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples for Git Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains shell command blocks and command substitution examples for git status, git diff, git add, and git commit. These are expected for the workflow, but agents must treat filenames and commit text carefully and avoid unreviewed staging.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are directly visible in Markdown command blocks. They are not hidden or obfuscated, but they can affect repository state when followed.

content.value_statement: Writing clear commit messages from a busy diff can slow reviews and releases. This skill inspects git changes, suggests Conventional Commits messages, and asks for confirmation before committing.
actual_capabilities: Runs git status and diff commands to inspect staged and unstaged changes.
Classifies changes using Conventional Commits types such as feat, fix, docs, test, and chore.
Drafts commit subjects, bodies, and breaking-change footers when appropriate.
Asks whether to stage all changes, selected files, or review the diff first.
Uses a heredoc pattern for multi-line commit messages.
Shows the latest commit and notes remaining unstaged changes after committing.
limitations: It depends on local git command output and cannot assess changes outside the repository.
It may need user guidance when several unrelated changes should be split.
It does not replace human review for secrets, generated files, or large binaries.
It should not amend pushed commits unless the user explicitly requests that action.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Feature Commit
use_cases.description: Turn staged feature work into a concise Conventional Commits message with an optional explanatory body.

use_cases.title: Split Mixed Changes
use_cases.description: Identify unrelated edits and suggest separate commit messages for each logical change.

use_cases.title: Document Breaking Changes
use_cases.description: Mark breaking API or behavior changes with the correct subject marker and footer text.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Commit Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my staged changes and suggest a Conventional Commits message. Show the message before committing.

prompt_templates.title: Stage and Commit Selected Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Check the current git status, help me choose which files to stage, then draft a commit message for those files.

prompt_templates.title: Split Unrelated Work
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for multiple logical changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Review all current changes and tell me whether they should be split into separate commits. Suggest the commit order and messages.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Breaking Change Commit
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the staged diff for breaking changes and prepare a Conventional Commits message with the correct marker and footer.

output_examples.output: A suggested feature commit for the auth scope, with a short subject and a body explaining token refresh behavior and test coverage.

output_examples.input: ready to commit the API response update
output_examples.output: A breaking-change commit recommendation that flags the API scope and includes migration guidance for clients.

output_examples.input: commit this mixed parser, export, and docs work
output_examples.output: A recommendation to split the work into one fix commit, one feature commit, and one documentation commit.

best_practices: Review the staged diff before allowing the skill to create a commit.
Split unrelated changes into separate commits with focused messages.
Check for secrets, generated files, and large binaries before staging.
anti_patterns: Do not commit every unstaged file without checking what changed.
Do not hide breaking changes in a generic chore or refactor commit.
Do not bypass hooks or amend pushed commits without an explicit reason.

faq.question: Does this skill create commits automatically?
faq.answer: It is designed to show the proposed message and ask for confirmation before running git commit.

faq.question: Which commit format does it use?
faq.answer: It uses Conventional Commits with type, optional scope, subject, body, and footer sections.

faq.question: Can it handle breaking changes?
faq.answer: Yes. It can suggest the breaking-change marker and footer when the diff changes public behavior or APIs.

faq.question: Can it split one working tree into multiple commits?
faq.answer: Yes. It can identify unrelated changes and suggest separate staging and commit steps.

faq.question: Does it check for secrets?
faq.answer: The skill tells the agent to check staged files for secrets, but users should still review sensitive changes manually.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill uses legitimate git commands, but staging files and creating commits can change repository state.
